%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
## Overview

> **Objective:** Build a reliable reference toolkit of mathematical formulas that appear repeatedly in signals and systems work.

Section B.8 closes the background chapter by collecting the most useful mathematical facts in one place. It covers constants like π and ε, complex number identities (Euler's formula, polar form), geometric and arithmetic sums, Taylor and Maclaurin series, power series for εˣ, sin, and cos, trigonometric identities, and common derivative and integral formulas.

After working through this section you will be able to quickly look up or recall the formula you need, recognize which form of an expression is most useful for a given problem, and avoid the most common algebraic traps that appear on exams.

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="generate_image" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
*🎨 Five formula groups from B.8 at a glance. Euler's identity (highlighted) is the most-tested single relationship.*
![Illustration](/generated/gptimage2-1777214207691-4883.png)

## Core Idea

The central thread running through B.8 is **Euler's formula**:

$$
e^{j\theta} = \cos\theta + j\sin\theta
$$

This single identity connects every major group in the appendix. The complex-number identities follow directly from it. The power series for \(e^x\), \(\sin x\), and \(\cos x\) are what make it provable. The trigonometric product-to-sum identities (e.g., \(\cos x \cos y = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(x-y)+\cos(x+y)]\)) are derived by multiplying Euler expressions together.

**Minimal example:** To combine \(a\cos x + b\sin x\) into a single sinusoid, write each trig function as the real or imaginary part of a complex exponential, then read off amplitude \(C = \sqrt{a^2+b^2}\) and phase \(\theta = \tan^{-1}(-b/a)\).

### EXAM TRAP

The imaginary part of \(e^{j\theta}\) is \(\sin\theta\), **not** \(j\sin\theta\). Carrying the extra \(j\) into a final answer is one of the most common one-mark errors on exams.

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- Euler's formula \(e^{j\theta} = \cos\theta + j\sin\theta\) is the single most-tested identity in the appendix.
- Trig product-to-sum identities and polar form both follow directly from Euler's formula.
- The imaginary part of \(e^{j\theta}\) is \(\sin\theta\), never \(j\sin\theta\) — a classic exam trap.

*With this formula toolkit in hand, the next sections apply these identities directly to analyzing sinusoidal signals and complex exponential inputs in linear systems.*
